claim: Imperial Japanese political language encoded the Emperor's divine sovereignty and the national body (kokutai) as sacred, demanding individual self-sacrifice in service of imperial survival as the highest moral obligation overriding personal survival.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Two well-attested public-domain rescripts directly encode collective-body sacrifice logic. Confidence capped pending passage-text verification against cited scholarly editions and chain expansion.

claim: Nazi political language represented the national community as a sacred collective body whose preservation could override individual life and ordinary pluralist correction.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Two real attributed passages now support the claim. Passage text requires verification against Baynes (1942) and Stackelberg & Winkle (2002) before advancing past source-review.

claim: The Grundgesetz placed human dignity beyond political revision, embedding a constitutional limit on collective-body claims as the structural foundation of the postwar German symbolic order.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Two complementary passages from the Grundgesetz support the claim: Art. 1(1) establishes the inviolability of human dignity; Art. 79(3) places it beyond amendment. Confidence is capped pending chain expansion and secondary-source corroboration.

claim: The Soviet symbolic order collapsed when decades of institutional rigidity exhausted the founding legitimacy claims of Marxism-Leninism, and Gorbachev's reform attempts accelerated institutional disintegration rather than restoring coherence, culminating in the formal dissolution of the USSR.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Gorbachev's own acknowledgment of systemic failure and the Alma-Ata dissolution text directly support the legitimacy-collapse reading. Confidence capped pending chain expansion and verification of passage text against cited editions.
